**Hanyu Pinyin**, or simply **pinyin** (**pīnyīn**), officially the **Chinese Phonetic Alphabet**, is the most common [romanization](/wiki/Romanization "Romanization") system for [Standard Chinese](/wiki/Standard_Chinese "Standard Chinese"). *Hanyu* ([simplified Chinese](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters "Simplified Chinese characters"): 汉语; [traditional Chinese](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters "Traditional Chinese characters"): 漢語) literally means '[Han](/wiki/Han_Chinese "Han Chinese") language'—that is, the Chinese language—while *pinyin* literally means 'spelled sounds'. Pinyin is the official romanization system used in China, Singapore, and Taiwan, and by the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations"). Its use has become common when transliterating Standard Chinese mostly regardless of region, though it is less ubiquitous in Taiwan. It is used to teach Standard Chinese, normally written with [Chinese characters](/wiki/Chinese_characters "Chinese characters"), to students in mainland China and Singapore. Pinyin is also used by various [input methods](/wiki/Chinese_input_method "Chinese input method") on computers and to [categorize entries](/wiki/Lexicographic_ordering "Lexicographic ordering") in some [Chinese dictionaries](/wiki/Chinese_dictionaries "Chinese dictionaries").

In pinyin, each Chinese syllable is spelled in terms of an optional [initial](/wiki/Initial_(linguistics) "Initial (linguistics)") and a [final](/wiki/Final_(linguistics) "Final (linguistics)"), each of which is represented by one or more letters. Initials are initial consonants, whereas finals are all possible combinations of medials ([semivowels](/wiki/Semivowels "Semivowels") coming before the vowel), a [nucleus](/wiki/Syllable_nucleus "Syllable nucleus") vowel, and [coda](/wiki/Syllable_coda "Syllable coda") (final vowel or consonant). [Diacritics](/wiki/Diacritic "Diacritic") are used to indicate the four [tones](/wiki/Tone_(linguistics) "Tone (linguistics)") found in Standard Chinese, though these are often omitted in various contexts, such as when spelling [Chinese names](/wiki/Chinese_name "Chinese name") in non-Chinese texts.

Hanyu Pinyin was developed in the 1950s by a group of Chinese linguists including [Wang Li](/wiki/Wang_Li_(linguist) "Wang Li (linguist)"), [Lu Zhiwei](/wiki/Lu_Zhiwei "Lu Zhiwei"), [Li Jinxi](/wiki/Li_Jinxi "Li Jinxi"), [Luo Changpei](/wiki/Luo_Changpei "Luo Changpei") and, particularly, [Zhou Youguang](/wiki/Zhou_Youguang "Zhou Youguang"), who has been called the "father of pinyin". They based their work in part on earlier [romanization systems](/wiki/Romanization_of_Chinese "Romanization of Chinese"). The system was originally promulgated at the Fifth Session of the [1st National People's Congress](/wiki/1st_National_People%27s_Congress "1st National People's Congress") in 1958, and has seen several rounds of revisions since. The [International Organization for Standardization](/wiki/International_Organization_for_Standardization "International Organization for Standardization") propagated Hanyu Pinyin as **ISO 7098** in 1982, and the United Nations began using it in 1986. Taiwan adopted Hanyu Pinyin as its official romanization system in 2009, replacing [Tongyong Pinyin](/wiki/Tongyong_Pinyin "Tongyong Pinyin").

### Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pinyin&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Background")]

[Matteo Ricci](/wiki/Matteo_Ricci "Matteo Ricci"), a [Jesuit](/wiki/Jesuit "Jesuit") missionary in China, wrote the first book that used the Latin alphabet to write Chinese, entitled *Xizi Qiji* (Hsi-tzŭ Ch'i-chi; 西字奇蹟; 'Miracle of Western Letters') and published in Beijing in 1605.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Twenty years later, fellow Jesuit [Nicolas Trigault](/wiki/Nicolas_Trigault "Nicolas Trigault") published 'Aid to the Eyes and Ears of Western Literati' (西儒耳目資; *Xīrú ěrmù zī*; *Hsi ju erh mu tzŭ*) in Hangzhou.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Neither book had any influence among the contemporary Chinese literati, and the romanizations they introduced primarily were useful for Westerners.[[3]](#cite_note-Joseph-3)

During the late Qing, the reformer Song Shu (1862–1910) proposed that China adopt a phonetic writing system. A student of the scholars [Yu Yue](/wiki/Yu_Yue "Yu Yue") and [Zhang Taiyan](/wiki/Zhang_Taiyan "Zhang Taiyan"), Song had observed the effect of the *[kana](/wiki/Kana "Kana")* syllabaries and [Western learning](/wiki/Western_learning "Western learning") during his visits to Japan.[*[which?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Avoid_weasel_words "Wikipedia:Avoid weasel words")*] While Song did not himself propose a transliteration system for Chinese, his discussion ultimately led to a proliferation of proposed schemes.[[3]](#cite_note-Joseph-3) The [Wade–Giles](/wiki/Wade%E2%80%93Giles "Wade–Giles") system was produced by [Thomas Wade](/wiki/Thomas_Francis_Wade "Thomas Francis Wade") in 1859, and further improved by [Herbert Giles](/wiki/Herbert_Giles "Herbert Giles"), presented in *[Chinese–English Dictionary](/wiki/A_Chinese%E2%80%93English_Dictionary "A Chinese–English Dictionary")* (1892). It was popular, and was used in English-language publications outside China until 1979.[[4]](#cite_note-4) In 1943, the US military tapped [Yale University](/wiki/Yale_University "Yale University") to develop [another romanization system for Mandarin Chinese](/wiki/Yale_romanization_of_Mandarin "Yale romanization of Mandarin") intended for pilots flying over China—much more than previous systems, the result appears very similar to modern Hanyu Pinyin.

### Development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pinyin&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Development")]

Hanyu Pinyin was designed by a group of mostly Chinese linguists, including [Wang Li](/wiki/Wang_Li_(linguist) "Wang Li (linguist)"), [Lu Zhiwei](/wiki/Lu_Zhiwei "Lu Zhiwei"), [Li Jinxi](/wiki/Li_Jinxi "Li Jinxi"), [Luo Changpei](/wiki/Luo_Changpei "Luo Changpei"), as well as [Zhou Youguang](/wiki/Zhou_Youguang "Zhou Youguang") (1906–2017), an economist by profession, as part of a Chinese government project in the 1950s. Zhou, often called "the father of pinyin",[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFox2017-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBranigan2008-8) worked as a banker in New York when he decided to return to China to help rebuild the country after the People's Republic was established. Earlier attempts to [romanize Chinese writing](/wiki/Romanization_of_Chinese "Romanization of Chinese") were mostly abandoned in 1944. Zhou became an economics professor in Shanghai, and when the [Ministry of Education](/wiki/Ministry_of_Education_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Ministry of Education of the People's Republic of China") created the Committee for the Reform of the Chinese Written Language in 1955, Premier [Zhou Enlai](/wiki/Zhou_Enlai "Zhou Enlai") assigned him the task of developing a new romanization system, despite the fact that he was not a linguist by trade.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFox2017-5)

Hanyu Pinyin incorporated different aspects from existing systems, including [Gwoyeu Romatzyh](/wiki/Gwoyeu_Romatzyh "Gwoyeu Romatzyh") (1928) [Latinxua Sin Wenz](/wiki/Latinxua_Sin_Wenz "Latinxua Sin Wenz") (1931) and the [diacritics](/wiki/Diacritic "Diacritic") from [bopomofo](/wiki/Bopomofo "Bopomofo") (1918).[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERohsenow200423-9) "I'm not the father of pinyin", Zhou said years later; "I'm the son of pinyin. It's [the result of] a long tradition from the later years of the Qing dynasty down to today. But we restudied the problem and revisited it and made it more perfect."[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBranigan2008-8)

An initial draft was authored in January 1956 by [Ye Laishi](/w/index.php?title=Ye_Laishi&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ye Laishi (page does not exist)"), Lu Zhiwei and Zhou Youguang. A revised Pinyin scheme was proposed by Wang Li, Lu Zhiwei and Li Jinxi, and became the main focus of discussion among the group of Chinese linguists in June 1956, forming the basis of Pinyin standard later after incorporating a wide range of feedback and further revisions.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWang1995-10) The first edition of Hanyu Pinyin was approved and officially adopted at the Fifth Session of the [1st National People's Congress](/wiki/1st_National_People%27s_Congress "1st National People's Congress") on 11 February 1958. It was then introduced to primary schools as a way to teach Standard Chinese pronunciation and used to improve the literacy rate among adults.[[11]](#cite_note-Asiaone-11)

Despite its formal promulgation, pinyin did not become widely used until after the tumult of the [Cultural Revolution](/wiki/Cultural_Revolution "Cultural Revolution").[[12]](#cite_note-:Mullaney-12): 189 In the 1980s, students were trained in pinyin from an early age, learning it in tandem with characters or even before.[[12]](#cite_note-:Mullaney-12): 200

During the height of the Cold War the use of pinyin system over Wade–Giles and [Yale romanizations](/wiki/Yale_romanization_of_Mandarin "Yale romanization of Mandarin") outside of China was regarded as a political statement or identification with the mainland Chinese government.[[13]](#cite_note-13) Beginning in the early 1980s, Western publications addressing mainland China began using the Hanyu Pinyin romanization system instead of earlier romanization systems; this change followed the [Joint Communiqué on the Establishment of Diplomatic Relations](/wiki/Joint_Communiqu%C3%A9_on_the_Establishment_of_Diplomatic_Relations "Joint Communiqué on the Establishment of Diplomatic Relations") between the United States and China in 1979.[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15) In 2001, the Chinese government issued the *National Common Language Law*, providing a legal basis for applying pinyin.[[11]](#cite_note-Asiaone-11) The current specification of the orthography is GB/T 16159–2012.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGB/T_161592012-16)

## Syllables

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pinyin&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Syllables")]

Chinese phonology is generally described in terms of initials (声母; 聲母; *shēngmǔ*) and finals (韵母; 韻母; *yùnmǔ*). This is distinct from the concept of consonant and vowel sounds as basic units in traditional (and most other phonetic systems used to describe the Chinese language). Every syllable in Standard Chinese can be described as a pair of one initial and one final, except for the special syllable *er* or when a trailing *-r* is considered part of a syllable (a phenomenon known as *[erhua](/wiki/Erhua "Erhua")*). The latter case, though a common practice in some sub-dialects, is rarely used in official publications.

While initials are usually a single consonant, finals are not always simple vowels, especially in compound finals (复韵母; 複韻母; *fùyùnmǔ*), i.e. when a "medial" is placed in front of the final. For example, the medials [[i](/wiki/Close_front_unrounded_vowel "Close front unrounded vowel")] and [[u](/wiki/Close_back_rounded_vowel "Close back rounded vowel")] are pronounced with such tight openings at the beginning of a final that some native Chinese speakers (especially when singing) pronounce *yī* (衣; 'clothes'), officially pronounced /í/, as /jí/ and *wéi* (围; 圍; 'to enclose'), officially pronounced /uěi/, as /wěi/ or /wuěi/. Often these medials are treated as separate from the finals rather than as part of them; this convention is followed in the chart of finals below.

They are spelled differently when there is no initial consonant in order to mark a new syllable: *fanguan* is *fan-guan*, while *fangwan* is *fang-wan* (and equivalent to *\*fang-uan)*. With this convention, an apostrophe only needs to be used to mark an initial *a* or *e*, e.g. *Xi'an* (two syllables: [ɕi.an]) vs. *xian* (one syllable: [ɕi̯ɛn]). In addition, *y* and *w* are added to fully vocalic *i, u*, and *ü* when these occur without an initial consonant, so that they are written *yi, wu*, and *yu*. Some Mandarin speakers do pronounce a [j] or [w] sound at the beginning of such words—that is, *yi* [i] or [ji], *wu* [u] or [wu], *yu* [y] or [ɥy],—so this is an intuitive convention. See below for a few finals which are abbreviated after a consonant plus *w/u* or *y/i* medial: *wen* → C+*un*, *wei* → C+*ui*, *weng* → C+*ong*, and *you* → Q+*iu*.

#### The ⟨ü⟩ sound

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pinyin&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: The ⟨ü⟩ sound")]

An [umlaut](/wiki/Umlaut_(diacritic) "Umlaut (diacritic)") is added to ⟨u⟩ when it occurs after the initials ⟨l⟩ and ⟨n⟩ when necessary in order to represent the sound [y]. This is necessary in order to distinguish the front high rounded vowel in *lü* (e.g. 驴; 驢; 'donkey') from the back high rounded vowel in *lu* (e.g. 炉; 爐; 'oven'). Tonal markers are placed above the umlaut, as in *lǘ*.

However, the *ü* is *not* used in the other contexts where it could represent a front high rounded vowel, namely after the letters *j*, *q*, *x*, and *y*. For example, the sound of the word for 'fish' (鱼; 魚) is transcribed in pinyin simply as *yú*, not as \**yǘ*. This practice is opposed to Wade–Giles, which always uses *ü*, and [Tongyong Pinyin](/wiki/Tongyong_Pinyin "Tongyong Pinyin"), which always uses *yu*. Whereas Wade–Giles needs the umlaut to distinguish between *chü* (pinyin *ju*) and *chu* (pinyin *zhu*), this ambiguity does not arise with pinyin, so the more convenient form *ju* is used instead of *jü*. Genuine ambiguities only happen with *nu*/*nü* and *lu*/*lü*, which are then distinguished by an umlaut.

Many fonts or output methods do not support an umlaut for *ü* or cannot place tone marks on top of *ü*. Likewise, using *ü* in input methods is difficult because it is not present as a simple key on many keyboard layouts. For these reasons *v* is sometimes used instead by convention. For example, it is common for cellphones to use *v* instead of *ü*. Additionally, some stores in China use *v* instead of *ü* in the transliteration of their names. The drawback is a lack of [precomposed characters](/wiki/Precomposed_character "Precomposed character") and limited font support for [combining accents](/wiki/Combining_accent "Combining accent") on the letter *v*, (v̄ v́ v̌ v̀).

This also presents a problem in transcribing names for use on passports, affecting people with names that consist of the sound *lü* or *nü*, particularly people with the surname 吕 (*[Lǚ](/wiki/L%C3%BC_(surname) "Lü (surname)")*), a fairly common surname, particularly compared to the surnames 陆 (*[Lù](/wiki/Lu_(surname_%E9%99%86) "Lu (surname 陆)")*), 鲁 (*[Lǔ](/wiki/Lu_(surname_%E9%B2%81) "Lu (surname 鲁)")*), 卢 (*[Lú](/wiki/Lu_(surname_%E5%8D%A2) "Lu (surname 卢)")*) and 路 (*[Lù](/wiki/Lu_(surname_%E8%B7%AF) "Lu (surname 路)")*). Previously, the practice varied among different passport issuing offices, with some transcribing as "LV" and "NV" while others used "LU" and "NU". On 10 July 2012, the [Ministry of Public Security](/wiki/Ministry_of_Public_Security_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Ministry of Public Security of the People's Republic of China") standardized the practice to use "LYU" and "NYU" in passports.[[18]](#cite_note-21)[[19]](#cite_note-22)

Although *nüe* written as *nue*, and *lüe* written as *lue* are not ambiguous, *nue* or *lue* are not correct according to the rules; *nüe* and *lüe* should be used instead. However, some Chinese input methods support both *nve*/*lve* (typing *v* for *ü*) and *nue*/*lue*.

## Tones

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pinyin&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Tones")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/Pinyin_Tone_Chart.svg/250px-Pinyin_Tone_Chart.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Pinyin_Tone_Chart.svg)

Relative pitch changes of the four tones

The pinyin system also uses four [diacritics](/wiki/Diacritic "Diacritic") to mark the [tones of Mandarin](/wiki/Tones_of_Mandarin "Tones of Mandarin").[[20]](#cite_note-23) In the pinyin system, four main tones of Mandarin are shown by diacritics: ā, á, ǎ, and à.[[21]](#cite_note-24) There is no symbol or diacritic for the neutral tone: a. The diacritic is placed over the letter that represents the [syllable nucleus](/wiki/Syllable_nucleus "Syllable nucleus"), unless that letter is missing. Tones are used in Hanyu Pinyin symbols, and they do not appear in Chinese characters.

Tones are written on the finals of Chinese pinyin. If the tone mark is written over an *i*, then it replaces the tittle, as in *yī*.

1. The first tone (flat or high-level tone) is represented by a [macron](/wiki/Macron_(diacritic) "Macron (diacritic)") ⟨ˉ⟩ added to the pinyin vowel:

   :   ā ē ê̄ ī ō ū ǖ Ā Ē Ê̄ Ī Ō Ū Ǖ
2. The second tone (rising or high-rising tone) is denoted by an [acute accent](/wiki/Acute_accent "Acute accent") ⟨ˊ⟩:

   :   á é ế í ó ú ǘ Á É Ế Í Ó Ú Ǘ
3. The third tone (falling-rising or low tone) is marked by a [caron](/wiki/Caron "Caron") ⟨ˇ⟩:

   :   ǎ ě ê̌ ǐ ǒ ǔ ǚ Ǎ Ě Ê̌ Ǐ Ǒ Ǔ Ǚ
4. The fourth tone (falling or high-falling tone) is represented by a [grave accent](/wiki/Grave_accent "Grave accent") ⟨ˋ⟩:

   :   à è ề ì ò ù ǜ À È Ề Ì Ò Ù Ǜ
5. The fifth tone (neutral tone) is represented by a normal vowel without any accent mark:

   :   a e ê i o u ü A E Ê I O U Ü

In dictionaries, neutral tone may be indicated by a dot preceding the syllable—e.g. *·ma*. When a neutral tone syllable has an alternative pronunciation in another tone, a combination of tone marks may be used: *zhī·dào* (知道) may be pronounced either *zhīdào* or *zhīdao*.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGB/T_161592012§7.3-25)

### Numbers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pinyin&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Numbers")]

Before the advent of computers, many typewriter fonts did not contain vowels with [macron](/wiki/Macron_(diacritic) "Macron (diacritic)") or [caron](/wiki/Caron "Caron") diacritics. Tones were thus represented by placing a [tone number](/wiki/Tone_number "Tone number") at the end of individual syllables. For example, *tóng* is written *tong2*. ### Placement and omission

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pinyin&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Placement and omission")]

Briefly, tone marks should always be placed in the order *a, e, o, i, u, ü*, with the only exception being *iu* where the tone mark is placed on the second vowel instead. Pinyin tone marks appear primarily above the syllable nucleus—e.g. as in *kuài*, where *k* is the initial, *u* the medial, *a* the nucleus, and *i* is the coda. There is an exception for syllabic nasals like /m/, where the nucleus of the syllable is a consonant: there, the diacritic will be carried by a written dummy vowel.

When the nucleus is /[ə](/wiki/Mid_central_vowel "Mid central vowel")/ (written *e* or *o*), and there is both a medial and a coda, the nucleus may be dropped from writing. In this case, when the coda is a consonant *n* or *ng*, the only vowel left is the medial *i, u*, or *ü*, and so this takes the diacritic. However, when the coda is a vowel, it is the coda rather than the medial which takes the diacritic in the absence of a written nucleus. This occurs with syllables ending in *-ui* (from *wei*: *wèi* → *-uì*) and in *-iu* (from *you*: *yòu* → *-iù*). That is, in the absence of a written nucleus the finals have priority for receiving the tone marker, as long as they are vowels; if not, the medial takes the diacritic.

An algorithm to find the correct vowel letter (when there is more than one) is as follows:

1. If there is an *a* or an *e*, it will take the tone mark
2. In the combination *ou*, then the *o* takes the tone mark
3. Otherwise, the second vowel takes the tone mark

Worded differently,

1. If there is an *a, e*, or *o*, it will take the tone mark; in the case of *ao*, the mark goes on the *a*
2. Otherwise, the vowels are *-iu* or *-ui*, in which case the second vowel takes the tone mark

The above can be summarized as the following table. The vowel letter taking the tone mark is indicated by the fourth-tone mark.

Placement of the pinyin tone mark

|  | -a | -e | -o | -i | -u |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| a- |  | | *ào* | *ài* |  |
| e- |  |  | |
| o- | èi | *òu* |
| i- | *ià*, *iào* | *iè* | *iò* |  | *iù* |
| u- | *uà*, *uài* | *uè* | *uò* | *uì* |  |
| ü- | *(üà)* | *üè* |  | | |

## Spacing, capitalization, and punctuation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pinyin&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Spacing, capitalization, and punctuation")]

Standard Chinese has many [polysyllabic](/wiki/Polysyllabic "Polysyllabic") words. Like in other writing systems using the Latin alphabet, spacing in pinyin is officially based on word boundaries. However, there are often ambiguities in partitioning a word. *The Basic Rules of the Chinese Phonetic Alphabet Orthography* were put into effect in 1988 by the National Educational and National Language commissions.[[23]](#cite_note-ortho-26) These rules became a GB recommendation in 1996,[[23]](#cite_note-ortho-26) and were last updated in 2012.[[24]](#cite_note-27)

In practice, however, published materials in China now often space pinyin syllable by syllable. According to [Victor H. Mair](/wiki/Victor_H._Mair "Victor H. Mair"), this practice became widespread after the Script Reform Committee, previously under direct control of the [State Council](/wiki/State_Council_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "State Council of the People's Republic of China"), had its power greatly weakened in 1985 when it was renamed the [State Language Commission](/wiki/State_Language_Commission "State Language Commission") and placed under the [Ministry of Education](/wiki/Ministry_of_Education_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Ministry of Education of the People's Republic of China").[[25]](#cite_note-28) Mair claims that proponents of Chinese characters in the educational bureaucracy "became alarmed that word-based pinyin was becoming a de facto alternative to Chinese characters as a script for writing Mandarin and demanded that all pinyin syllables be written separately."[[26]](#cite_note-29)

## Comparison with other orthographies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pinyin&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Comparison with other orthographies")]

Pinyin superseded older romanization systems such as Wade–Giles and [postal romanization](/wiki/Postal_romanization "Postal romanization"), and replaced bopomofo as the method of Chinese phonetic instruction in [mainland China](/wiki/Mainland_China "Mainland China"). The ISO adopted pinyin as the standard romanization for modern Chinese in 1982 (ISO 7098:1982, superseded by ISO 7098:2015). The [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") followed suit in 1986.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFox2017-5)[[27]](#cite_note-lin-30) It has also been accepted by the [government of Singapore](/wiki/Government_of_Singapore "Government of Singapore"), the United States's [Library of Congress](/wiki/Library_of_Congress "Library of Congress"), the [American Library Association](/wiki/American_Library_Association "American Library Association"), and many other international institutions.[[28]](#cite_note-31)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

Compared to Wade–Giles, pinyin more accurately reflects the aspiration distinction for English speakers, but assigns some Latin letters sound values which are quite different from those of most languages.[[29]](#cite_note-32) This has drawn some criticism as it may lead to confusion when uninformed speakers apply either native or English assumed pronunciations to words. However, this problem is not limited to pinyin, since many languages that use the Latin alphabet natively also assign different values to the same letters. A 2014 overview of topics in Chinese writing and literacy claimed, "By and large, pinyin represents the Chinese sounds better than the Wade–Giles system, and does so with fewer extra marks.".[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylorTaylor2014124-33)

As pinyin is a phonetic writing system for modern [Standard Chinese](/wiki/Standard_Chinese "Standard Chinese"), it is not designed to replace characters for writing [Literary Chinese](/wiki/Literary_Chinese "Literary Chinese"), the standard written language prior to the early 20th century. In particular, Chinese characters retain semantic cues that help distinguish differently pronounced words in the ancient classical language that are now [homophones](/wiki/Homophone "Homophone") in Mandarin. Thus, Chinese characters remain indispensable for recording and transmitting the corpus of Chinese writing from the past.

Pinyin is not designed to transcribe [varieties](/wiki/Chinese_language_varieties "Chinese language varieties") other than Standard Chinese, which is based on the phonological system of Beijing Mandarin. Other romanization schemes have been devised to transcribe those other Chinese varieties, such as [Jyutping](/wiki/Jyutping "Jyutping") for Cantonese and [Pe̍h-ōe-jī](/wiki/Pe%CC%8Dh-%C5%8De-j%C4%AB "Pe̍h-ōe-jī") for [Hokkien](/wiki/Hokkien "Hokkien").

## Typography and encoding

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pinyin&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Typography and encoding")]

Based on the "Chinese Romanization" section of ISO 7098:2015, pinyin tone marks should use the symbols from [Combining Diacritical Marks](/wiki/Combining_Diacritical_Marks "Combining Diacritical Marks"), as opposed by the use of [Spacing Modifier Letters](/wiki/Spacing_Modifier_Letters "Spacing Modifier Letters") in bopomofo. Lowercase letters with tone marks are included in [GB 2312](/wiki/GB_2312 "GB 2312") and their uppercase counterparts are included in [JIS X 0212](/wiki/JIS_X_0212 "JIS X 0212");[[31]](#cite_note-34) thus Unicode includes all the common accented characters from pinyin.[[32]](#cite_note-thetype-pinyin-35) Other punctuation mark and symbols in Chinese are to use the equivalent symbol in English noted in to GB 15834.

According to GB 16159, all accented letters are required to have both uppercase and lowercase characters as per their normal counterparts.

It is commonly recommended that pinyin annotations use a typeface with a single-story 'a' (ɑ) and 'g' (g) and a second-tone mark (◌́) that is thicker at the bottom. However, there is no official restriction on the form of 'a' and 'g'.[[33]](#cite_note-36)

## Usage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pinyin&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Usage")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/Dajia-shuo-Putonghua-2817.jpg/330px-Dajia-shuo-Putonghua-2817.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dajia-shuo-Putonghua-2817.jpg)

A slogan written on a school wall featuring pinyin annotations without tonal marks or word grouping

The spelling of Chinese geographical or personal names in pinyin has become the most common way to transcribe them in English. Pinyin has also become the dominant [Chinese input method](/wiki/Chinese_input_method "Chinese input method") in mainland China, in contrast to Taiwan, where bopomofo is most commonly used.

Families outside of Taiwan who speak Mandarin as a mother tongue use pinyin to help children associate characters with spoken words which they already know. Children start to learn it in kindergarten, but pinyin disappears from textbooks after primary school.[[34]](#cite_note-37) Chinese families outside of Taiwan who speak some other language as their mother tongue use the system to teach children Mandarin pronunciation when learning vocabulary in elementary school.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESnowlingHulme2005320–322-38)

Since 1958, pinyin has been actively used in [adult education](/wiki/Adult_education "Adult education") as well, making it easier for formerly illiterate people to continue with self-study after a short period of pinyin literacy instruction.[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPrice2005206–208-39)

Pinyin has become a tool for many non-native speakers to learn Mandarin pronunciation, and is used to explain both the grammar and spoken Mandarin coupled with [Chinese characters](/wiki/Chinese_characters "Chinese characters"). Books containing both Chinese characters and pinyin are often used by non-native speakers learning Chinese. Pinyin's role in teaching pronunciation to non-native speakers and children is similar in some respects to *[furigana](/wiki/Furigana "Furigana")*-based books with *[hiragana](/wiki/Hiragana "Hiragana")* letters written alongside [kanji](/wiki/Kanji "Kanji") (directly analogous to bopomofo) in Japanese, or fully vocalized texts in Arabic.

The tone-marking diacritics are commonly omitted in popular news stories and even in scholarly works, as well as in the traditional [Mainland Chinese Braille](/wiki/Mainland_Chinese_Braille "Mainland Chinese Braille") system, which is similar to pinyin, but meant for blind readers.[[37]](#cite_note-40) This results in some degree of ambiguity as to which words are being represented.

### Computer input

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pinyin&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Computer input")]

Simple computer systems, sometimes only able to use simple character systems for text, such as the 7-bit [ASCII](/wiki/ASCII "ASCII") standard—essentially the 26 Latin letters, 10 digits, and punctuation marks—long provided a convincing argument for using unaccented pinyin instead of diacritical pinyin or Chinese characters. Today, however, most computer systems are able to display characters from Chinese and many other writing systems as well, and have them entered with a Latin keyboard using an [input method](/wiki/Input_method "Input method") editor. Alternatively, some touchscreen devices allow users to input characters graphically by writing with a [stylus](/wiki/Stylus "Stylus"), with concurrent online [handwriting recognition](/wiki/Handwriting_recognition "Handwriting recognition").

Pinyin with accents can be entered with the use of special keyboard layouts or various other utilities.

### Sorting techniques

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pinyin&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Sorting techniques")]

Main article: [Pinyin alphabetical order](/wiki/Pinyin_alphabetical_order "Pinyin alphabetical order")

Chinese text can be sorted by its pinyin representation, which is often useful for looking up words whose pronunciations are known, but not whose character forms are not known. Chinese characters and words can be sorted for convenient lookup by their Pinyin expressions alphabetically.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWangZou200326–27-41) Identical syllables are then further sorted by tone number, ascending, with neutral tones placed last.

Words of multiple characters can be sorted in two different ways,[[39]](#cite_note-42) either per character, as is used in the *[Xiandai Hanyu Cidian](/wiki/Xiandai_Hanyu_Cidian "Xiandai Hanyu Cidian")*, or by the whole word's string, which is only then sorted by tone. This method is used in the *[ABC Chinese–English Dictionary](/wiki/ABC_Chinese%E2%80%93English_Dictionary "ABC Chinese–English Dictionary")*.

### By region

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pinyin&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: By region")]

#### Taiwan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pinyin&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Taiwan")]

See also: [Chinese language romanization in Taiwan](/wiki/Chinese_language_romanization_in_Taiwan "Chinese language romanization in Taiwan") and [Tongyong Pinyin](/wiki/Tongyong_Pinyin "Tongyong Pinyin")

Between October 2002 and January 2009, Taiwan used [Tongyong Pinyin](/wiki/Tongyong_Pinyin "Tongyong Pinyin"), a domestic modification of Hanyu Pinyin, as its official romanization system. Thereafter, it began to promote the use of Hanyu Pinyin instead. Tongyong Pinyin was designed to romanize varieties spoken on the island in addition to Standard Chinese. The ruling [Kuomintang](/wiki/Kuomintang "Kuomintang") (KMT) party resisted its adoption, preferring the system by then used in mainland China and internationally. Romanization preferences quickly became associated with issues of national identity. Preferences split along party lines: the KMT and its affiliated parties in the Pan-Blue Coalition supported the use of Hanyu Pinyin while the [Democratic Progressive Party](/wiki/Democratic_Progressive_Party "Democratic Progressive Party") (DPP) and its allies in the Pan-Green Coalition favored the use of Tongyong Pinyin.

Today, many street signs in Taiwan use Tongyong Pinyin or derived romanizations,[[40]](#cite_note-43) but some use Hanyu Pinyin–derived romanizations. It is not unusual to see spellings on street signs and buildings derived from the older Wade–Giles, [MPS2](/wiki/MPS2 "MPS2") and other systems. Attempts to make Hanyu Pinyin standard in Taiwan have had uneven success, with most place and proper names remaining unaffected, including all major cities. Personal names on Taiwanese passports honor the choices of Taiwanese citizens, who can choose Wade–Giles, Hakka, [Hoklo](/wiki/Hoklo_Taiwanese "Hoklo Taiwanese"), Tongyong, aboriginal, or pinyin.[[41]](#cite_note-44) Official use of pinyin is controversial, as when pinyin use for a metro line in 2017 provoked protests, despite government responses that "The romanization used on road signs and at transportation stations is intended for foreigners ... Every foreigner learning Mandarin learns Hanyu pinyin, because it is the international standard ... The decision has nothing to do with the nation's self-determination or any ideologies, because the key point is to ensure that foreigners can read signs."[[42]](#cite_note-45)

#### Singapore

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pinyin&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Singapore")]

See also: [Chinese language romanization in Singapore](/wiki/Chinese_language_romanization_in_Singapore "Chinese language romanization in Singapore")

[Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore") implemented Hanyu Pinyin as the official romanization system for Mandarin in the public sector starting in the 1980s, in conjunction with the [Speak Mandarin Campaign](/wiki/Speak_Mandarin_Campaign "Speak Mandarin Campaign").[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBockhorst-HengLee20073-46) Hanyu Pinyin is also used as the romanization system to teach Mandarin Chinese at schools.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChan2016485-47) While adoption has been mostly successful in government communication, placenames, and businesses established in the 1980s and onward, it continues to be unpopular in some areas, most notably for personal names and vocabulary borrowed from other varieties of Chinese already established in the local vernacular.[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBockhorst-HengLee20073-46) In these situations, romanization continues to be based on the Chinese language variety it originated from, especially the three largest Chinese varieties traditionally spoken in Singapore: [Hokkien](/wiki/Hokkien "Hokkien"), [Teochew](/wiki/Teochew_dialect "Teochew dialect"), and [Cantonese](/wiki/Cantonese "Cantonese").

### Special names

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pinyin&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Special names")]

See also: [SASM/GNC romanization](/wiki/SASM/GNC_romanization "SASM/GNC romanization"), [Tibetan pinyin](/wiki/Tibetan_pinyin "Tibetan pinyin"), and [Guangdong Romanization](/wiki/Guangdong_Romanization "Guangdong Romanization")

In accordance to the *Regulation of Phonetic Transcription in Hanyu Pinyin Letters of Place Names in Minority Nationality Languages* (少数民族语地名汉语拼音字母音译转写法; 少數民族語地名漢語拼音字母音譯寫法) promulgated in 1976, place names in non-Han languages like [Mongolian](/wiki/Mongolian_language "Mongolian language"), [Uyghur](/wiki/Uyghur_language "Uyghur language"), and [Tibetan](/wiki/Standard_Tibetan "Standard Tibetan") are also officially transcribed using pinyin in a system adopted by the State Administration of Surveying and Mapping and Geographical Names Committee known as [SASM/GNC romanization](/wiki/SASM/GNC_romanization "SASM/GNC romanization"). The pinyin letters (26 Roman letters, plus ⟨ü⟩ and ⟨ê⟩) are used to approximate the non-Han language in question as closely as possible. This results in spellings that are different from both the customary spelling of the place name, and the pinyin spelling of the name in Chinese:

| [Lhasa](/wiki/Lhasa_(city) "Lhasa (city)") | Lhasa | 拉萨; 拉薩; *Lāsà* |

| [Golmud](/wiki/Golmud "Golmud") | Golmud | 格尔木; 格爾木; *Gé'ěrmù* |
| [Qiqihar](/wiki/Qiqihar "Qiqihar") | Qiqihar | 齐齐哈尔; 齊齊哈爾; *Qíqíhā'ěr* |
